ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for fried pig skin as a food product; SNOMED CT coverage for processed pork snack items is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical small serving of fried pig skin is approximately 30g, equivalent to a small handful of crispy pieces consumed as a snack or side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore Food Composition Database — Fried Pig Skin (CR ID: F06090007)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: All core macros and reported micronutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) are taken directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database canonical entry for fried pig skin (CR ID: F06090007) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (14.5g/100g) is cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pork rinds (NDB No. 10928) as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown; this adaptation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0g per 100g)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Fried pig skin contains 0.0g carbohydrate per 100g per HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (CR ID: F06090007). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates; no GI measurement is possible or meaningful. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition. This treatment is consistent with the Project Veritas v2 rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content at 1439mg per 100g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension must avoid fried pig skin entirely; a single 30g serving delivers approximately 432mg sodium, representing over 70% of the recommended 600mg daily sodium limit for hypertensive patients, and regular consumption will significantly elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High saturated fat, estimated trans fat, and extreme caloric density worsening insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid fried pig skin due to its high saturated fat (~14.5g/100g) and estimated trans fat (~0.5g/100g) from deep-frying, both of which directly worsen insulin resistance, and its extreme caloric density of 593 kcal/100g which promotes weight gain and metabolic dysfunction.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and extreme sodium content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid fried pig skin due to its combination of high saturated fat (~14.5g/100g), estimated trans fat (~0.5g/100g), cholesterol (72mg/100g), and extreme sodium (1439mg/100g), all of which contribute to atherogenesis, elevated LDL cholesterol, endothelial dysfunction, and hypertensive cardiovascular stress.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory profile: deep-frying AGEs, trans fat, high saturated fat, extreme sodium, and arachidonic acid from pork
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid fried pig skin entirely as it triggers multiple pro-inflammatory hard gates including deep-frying-generated AGEs, estimated trans fat (~0.5g/100g), saturated fat far exceeding the 5g threshold, sodium at 1439mg/100g worsening tissue swelling, and arachidonic acid from pork contributing to inflammatory cascade.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried pig skin, also known as pork rinds or pork crackling, is a deep-fried snack made from the skin of the pig. The skin is cleaned, dried, and then submerged in hot oil until it puffs up and turns golden brown, resulting in an extremely crispy, airy, and crunchy texture with a rich, savory, and slightly fatty flavor. It is commonly consumed as a snack or side dish across Chinese, Southeast Asian, and Western cuisines. In Singapore, it is a popular snack food found in hawker centres and provision shops. Nutritionally, fried pig skin is very high in protein (58.4g/100g) due to the collagen-rich nature of pork skin, and high in fat (39.88g/100g) including significant saturated fat (~14.5g/100g) and estimated trans fat (~0.5g/100g) from the deep-frying process. Carbohydrate content is effectively zero (0.0g/100g), making it a negligible-carbohydrate food for which glycemic index is not applicable. Sodium content is extremely high at 1439mg/100g due to seasoning and processing. Caloric density is very high at 593 kcal/100g. Clinically, this food is contraindicated for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis due to its extreme sodium, saturated fat, trans fat, and pro-inflammatory profile. It is not suitable for heart-healthy or sodium-restricted diets.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in fried pig skin?
Can I eat pork rinds with high blood pressure?
Is fried pig skin high in protein?
Is pork crackling bad for cholesterol?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database canonical entry for fried pig skin (CR ID: F06090007) with a confidence of 0.95. HPB is an approved primary regional source for Singapore foods. Saturated fat value of ~14.5g/100g is cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy pork rind data as a secondary approved source since HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown. Trans fat estimate of ~0.5g/100g is derived from standard deep-fried pork skin composition profiles and is flagged as an estimate in notes.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Food Composition Database — fried pig skin contains no carbohydrates as the skin is composed almost entirely of protein (collagen) and fat with no starch or sugar content. Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as animal skin contains no dietary fibre. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0g/100g); gi_category set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_index set to null per Project Veritas v2 rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown and full micronutrient panel for this specific fried pig skin preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. Trans_fat_g of 0.5g/100g is an estimate based on standard deep-frying trans fat generation profiles for pork skin; this is not directly reported by HPB and is flagged as an estimate. Saturated_fat_g of 14.5g/100g is adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy pork rinds (NDB No. 10928) as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown; this adaptation is documented in nutrient_source. This food is not halal as it is derived from pork.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: All four core macros present and numeric: energy_kcal 593.0, protein_g 58.4, fat_g 39.88, carbohydrate_g 0.0. Carbohydrate zero is justified in notes as confirmed zero per HPB. Nutrient source is HPB Singapore Food Composition Database, an approved primary regional source, with URL pointing to the official HPB food composition database page. Saturated fat cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (approved secondary source), adaptation documented.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 0.0 (naturally zero, documented in notes), sodium_mg 1439.0, saturated_fat_g 14.5 (adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, documented), sugar_g 0.0 (naturally zero, documented). All four required secondary nutrients present as numeric values.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to null (not 0) as food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0g/100g) per Project Veritas v2 critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. gi_category set to 'not_applicable'. glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0. gi_source title and notes clearly document the rationale without citing a non-approved GI source. G2 compliance maintained: no invalid GI source organization is cited.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI provided for pork rind food product (FOODON_03302757). FoodEx2 code A00LC for pig skin fried provided. SNOMED CT null with documented gap note. At least one defensible ontology anchor present.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, explains what the food is, preparation method (deep-frying), taste/texture (crispy, airy, savory), cultural context (Singapore hawker centres), clinical relevance (contraindicated for hypertension, T2D, CVD, RA), and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern), Type 2 Diabetes (high concern, human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' correct), Cardiovascular Disease (high concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. Rheumatoid arthritis included as additional condition.

English variants (Fried pig skin, Fried pork skin, Pork crackling, Pork rinds, Crispy pork skin, Deep-fried pork skin), Chinese (炸猪皮, 炸豬皮), Malay (Kulit babi goreng), Tamil (வறுத்த பன்றி தோல்), Hindi (तला हुआ सूअर की खाल), romanized (Zha zhu pi) — all present in flat aliases. Consistency check passed.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Fixed g2_approved_source_compliance failure by correcting nutrient_source URL to point to the official HPB food composition database page (https://www.hpb.gov.sg/food-beverage/food-composition-database) and clarifying the source title to explicitly name the Singapore Food Composition Database.

Iteration 3: Fixed q4_glycemic_completeness failure — glycemic_index was already null (correct) but validator flagged it as non-numeric. Confirmed null is the correct value per the critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Revised gi_source to remove any appearance of citing a non-approved organization, setting organization to 'N/A' and ensuring the source_match_notes clearly explain the rationale without referencing an invalid GI database.
Iteration 3: Fixed q2 nutrient source compliance by ensuring the nutrient_source organization and URL are unambiguously from an approved source (HPB Singapore) with a valid accessible URL.
Iteration 3: Verified all aliases_by_language strings appear verbatim in flat aliases array — consistency confirmed.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Fried Pig Skin record passes all six hard gates and all eight Q criteria at iteration 3. Nutrient data sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (approved primary source, confidence 0.95) with saturated fat cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (approved secondary source). Mandatory Singaporean multilingual names present (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Zero carbohydrate confirmed and justified. GI correctly set to null with not_applicable category; gi_source cites N/A with clear rationale, avoiding any invalid GI source citation. All -1 fields documented in notes. Health context covers all three required conditions with specific clinical guidance and correct human_condition_name for Type 2 Diabetes. Aliases consistent between flat array and structured object.
